mission.title: Install and Configure Keycloak on Kubernetes
mission.description: Keycloak is an open-source Identity and Access Management solution that allows you to add authentication to applications and secure services with minimal effort. Installing Keycloak will enable you to manage user identities and access control effectively.

steps.title: Add the Keycloak Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Keycloak Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add keycloak https://www.keycloak.org/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Keycloak using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Keycloak using Helm with the specified version and create a namespace:
```bash
helm install keycloak keycloak/keycloak --version 26.5.4 --namespace keycloak --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Keycloak pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace keycloak
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access Keycloak
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Keycloak admin console:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/keycloak 8080:80 --namespace keycloak
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure Keycloak runs efficiently, you can set resource limits in the Helm values. Create a `values.yaml` file with the following content:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "1Gi"
``` 
Then upgrade your installation with:
```bash
helm upgrade keycloak keycloak/keycloak --version 26.5.4 --namespace keycloak -f values.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Keycloak will show the Keycloak pods in a 'Running' state when you run `kubectl get pods`. You should also be able to access the Keycloak admin console at `http://localhost:8080` after setting up port forwarding.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add keycloak https://www.keycloak.org/helm-charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Keycloak release using Helm to remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes:
```bash
helm uninstall keycloak --namespace keycloak
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd keycloaks.keycloak.org
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace keycloak
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Keycloak namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace keycloak
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Keycloak configuration and any necessary data:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace keycloak -o yaml > keycloak-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Keycloak to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade keycloak keycloak/keycloak --version 26.5.5 --namespace keycloak
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Keycloak pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace keycloak
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue may occur if there are misconfigurations or resource limits are too low. Check the logs for the pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace keycloak
``` 
Fix any configuration issues and consider increasing resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Keycloak service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Keycloak service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and check the port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace keycloak
kubectl port-forward svc/keycloak 8080:80 --namespace keycloak
``` 
Verify that the correct ports are being forwarded.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the upgrade, ensure that you are using a compatible version. Check the current version:
```bash
helm list --namespace keycloak
``` 
Then specify a compatible version in your upgrade command.

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume claims not deleted
troubleshooting.description:
If PVCs are not deleted after uninstalling, manually delete them:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace keycloak
kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name> --namespace keycloak
``` 
Ensure that the PVCs are not in use by any other resources.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
